The present report contains the budget performance and the budget for the United Nations Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO) for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, respectively.

The reporting period was marked by significant changes in posture following the capture of Goma by the Alliance Fleuve Congo/Mouvement du 23 mars (AFC/M23) in January 2025. In government-controlled areas, the Mission continued to protect civilians; provide support to the Luanda and Nairobi processes; strengthen State institutions; monitor human rights and combat impunity; support the national disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and stabilization programme; and advance security sector reform and governance. In areas where AFC/M23 established parallel administrations, in line with established standard operating procedures governing the protection of civilians seeking protection at United Nations facilities, MONUSCO provided physical protection to civilians and disarmed Congolese defence and security forces sheltering in mission premises; exercised its good offices in support of peace processes; and monitored human rights.

During the 2026/27 period, MONUSCO will continue to contribute to the protection of civilians in its areas of deployment and the achievement of the objectives set out in Security Council resolution 2773 (2025); and to support the stabilization and strengthening of State institutions in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. In addition to its continuing support for ongoing regional and international peace efforts, under Security Council resolution 2808 (2025) the Mission was formally authorized to support the implementation of a permanent ceasefire.

Financial resources

	MONUSCO incurred $893.1 million in expenditure for the 2024/25 period, representing a resource utilization rate of 98.4 per cent, compared with $1,000.4 million in expenditure and a resource utilization rate of 94.0 per cent in the 2023/24 period.

The unencumbered balance of $14.9 million reflects the net impact of (a) higher actual vacancy rates for uniformed personnel, in part due to restrictions on the deployment of personnel as the result of a hiring freeze; (b) a lower number of hours flown as a result of the closure of the Goma airport and surrounding airspace; and (c) delays in the implementation of programmatic activities, all as a result of the security situation in North Kivu since January 2025. The reduced requirements were offset in part by the payment of evacuation allowances to civilian personnel and their dependants and an increase in the salary scale for national staff.

The budget proposed for the 2026/27 period in an amount of $874.8 million represents an increase of $5.7 million, or 0.7 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $869.1 million approved for the 2025/26 period.

While the Mission initially reduced its budget proposal by $94.0 million, or 10.8 per cent, compared with the 2025/26 period owing to efficiencies and cost reduction measures related to the reduction in military and police personnel and their equipment, as well as efficiencies in civilian personnel and operational costs, the final budget proposal represents an increase of $5.7 million or 0.7 per cent for the 2026/27 period owing to the requirement to provide logistical support for the implementation of the new ceasefire monitoring mandate.

text: I.	Mandate, results achieved and planned outcome

children.text: A.	Mandate

1.	The mandate of the United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO) was established by the Security Council in its resolution 1925 (2010). The mandate for the performance period was provided in its resolutions 2717 (2023) and 2765 (2024). The most recent extension of the mandate was authorized by the Council in its resolution 2808 (2025), by which it extended the mandate until 20 December 2026.
2.	The Mission is mandated to help the Security Council to achieve the overall objective of advancing peace and security in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
B.	Budget implementation for 2024/25

3.	The budget performance for 2024/2025 reflects significant changes in posture for MONUSCO. At the beginning of the period, the Mission pursued its mandate in line with the planned trajectory, building on the disengagement from South Kivu; anchoring joint monitoring mechanisms for the Government’s road map for transition in the province; and supporting the United Nations transition plan.

4.	This trajectory shifted early in 2025, following the rapid advance of the Alliance Fleuve Congo/Mouvement du 23 mars (AFC/M23) towards Goma and Bukavu. The collapse of State authority in large parts of North Kivu, coupled with widespread human and material losses, forced MONUSCO into a crisis posture. Under intense bombardment affecting the city of Goma, MONUSCO premises became a place of refuge for thousands, including members of the national security forces, human rights defenders, public officials and civilians. Notwithstanding grave risks to its personnel, the Mission maintained its protection role, safeguarding more than 3,000 individuals in Goma, facilitating the transfer of 1,359 unarmed security personnel to Kinshasa with support from the International Committee of the Red Cross, and ensuring the evacuation of non-essential United Nations staff and dependents from Goma and Bukavu.

5.	The AFC/M23 offensive posed direct threats to United Nations compounds, with hundreds of staff in Goma forced to shelter in place. The security situation affected the freedom of movement of MONUSCO, which required the Mission to adopt a two-track operational approach to implementing its mandate. In government-controlled areas, the Mission continued to protect civilians; strengthen State institutions; support the national disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and stabilization programme; advance security sector reform and governance; and promote national cohesion. In areas where AFC/M23 established parallel administrations, MONUSCO provided physical protection to civilians sheltering in MONUSCO premises; engaged regularly with the de facto authorities to protect and ensure respect for fundamental human rights and humanitarian law; enhanced coordination with humanitarian organizations and the country team on core civilian protection requirements; maintained its human rights monitoring and good offices functions; and supported dialogue to advance political solutions to the conflict.

6.	The Mission continued to focus on the protection of civilians, leveraging its civilian, police and military capacities through proactive deployment and confidence-building measures and by fostering a protective environment. The deployment and subsequent withdrawal of the Southern African Development Community (SADC) Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo altered the regional security architecture, requiring MONUSCO to recalibrate its posture.

7.	The Mission’s protection work remained multidimensional. In parts of North Kivu and Ituri, MONUSCO maintained its three-tiered approach to the protection of civilians, expanding early warning networks and responding to 1,231 alerts. A total of 7,532 incidents of human rights violations were documented and reported, while 624 illegally detained persons were released as a result of MONUSCO advocacy and prison monitoring. The Mission supported 18 mobile courts, enabling the sentencing of 195 accused, including 82 members of the Armed Forces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (FARDC), 19 police officers, 48 members of armed groups and 46 civilians. In addition, 24 joint investigation missions were conducted to combat impunity. In areas controlled by AFC/M23, MONUSCO recalibrated its protection efforts by integrating human rights monitoring and reporting; enhancing the operational capacity of networks of human rights defenders; and relocating those at risk. In an effort to reverse the rise of hate speech resulting from the territorial expansion of AFC/M23, MONUSCO launched a nationwide campaign to promote social cohesion, reaching 18 million Congolese in cities and rural communities in the span of four months.

8.	The Joint Operations and Coordination Centre established in Bunia continued to demonstrate its utility, responding to alerts, on average, within 15 minutes. The Mission helped to protect women at risk, including survivors of conflict-related sexual violence. Two projects provided medical care, psychological support, legal aid and socioeconomic assistance to 495 victims, of whom 477 were women. The projects also helped to strengthen the capacity of the Bukavu forensic centre in handling cases of sexual violence and to ensure the operation of the national toll-free helpline to report gender-based violence. In areas under the control of non-State armed groups, MONUSCO adopted a comprehensive approach that integrated protection, human rights monitoring, situational analysis and mediation, in order to lay the foundation for future stabilization. MONUSCO continued to support community alert networks covering 1,492 villages through the training of 3,247 persons, including 947 women in North Kivu and Ituri.

9.	The Mission conducted 6,905 patrols during the reporting period, including joint patrols with the Congolese National Police, and responded to 11 incidents related to improvised explosive devices. MONUSCO provided technical advice to national authorities in drafting a national strategy to counter improvised explosive devices and provided specialized training on explosive ordnance threat mitigation and weapons and ammunition management to 541 national security personnel. In addition, MONUSCO provided explosive ordnance risk education to 2,149 community members, conducted 301 spot tasks to dispose of or mark explosive remnants of war, and disposed of 2,392 weapons and 12,065 weapons components surrendered by defence and security forces when seeking refuge on MONUSCO premises.

10.	In the political sphere, MONUSCO demonstrated the unique value of its good offices. The visit by the Special Representative of the Secretary-General to Goma during the M23/AFC occupation exemplified the Mission’s determination to pursue principled engagement and mandate implementation in a complex environment, with a focus on protecting affected populations.

11.	The Mission continued to provide political and technical support to the Luanda and Nairobi processes, which shaped the main pillars of the Peace Agreement between the Democratic Republic of the Congo and the Republic of Rwanda (Washington Peace Agreement) signed in June 2025, in which the parties agree to implement the harmonized plan for the neutralization of the Forces démocratiques de libération du Rwanda (FDLR) and the disengagement of forces and the lifting of defensive measures by Rwanda of 31 October 2024. Those efforts supported an inclusive process involving regular engagements with the Government, as well as community, religious, youth and women leaders, on pathways to strengthen national cohesion, sustain confidence in peace efforts and address the drivers of conflict.

12.	The Mission also focused on strengthening local peace and mediation capacities. In Ituri, the permanent dialogue mechanism has been institutionalized as a locally owned mechanism to monitor peace agreements, structure peace efforts and engage relevant stakeholders, including armed groups. MONUSCO supported the creation of the Ituri interfaith network which developed a targeted road map prioritizing inter-community conflicts in Djugu and Irumu. Women’s participation was reinforced through a network of 100 women mediators active in Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu. The network helped de-escalate conflict and facilitated care for 1,000 survivors of conflict-related sexual violence through an early warning system. MONUSCO also deepened its strategic partnership with the peacebuilding community of practice in order to promote the long-term role of local peace actors in supporting State institutions and sustaining stabilization gains.

13.	MONUSCO continued to assist the Government in implementing the disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and stabilization programme at the national and provincial levels. The Mission funded reinsertion projects in Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu, benefiting more than 10,000 ex-combatants and community members, including 2,500 women. These projects, which were implemented with international and local partners, promoted reintegration through labour-intensive initiatives, improving infrastructure, building trust and fostering dialogue, resilience and women’s empowerment. A rapid response mechanism enabled psychosocial support and community dialogue in Ituri and strengthened the provincial permanent dialogue mechanism to monitor peace agreements and promote reconciliation. In addition, community violence-reduction initiatives reached 2,393 beneficiaries, of whom 779 were women, including 640 ex-combatants; 724 vulnerable community members and children formerly associated with armed groups; and 1,029 at-risk young people. A joint initiative of the disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and stabilization programme, the United Nations country team and MONUSCO was launched in May 2025 to provide reinsertion support to 1,130 Zaïre ex-combatants demobilized in January 2025. To reinforce long-term reintegration, MONUSCO developed an information, counselling and referral system to identify socioeconomic opportunities. Piloted in Tanganyika and expanded to South Kivu, the system was handed over to the national programme in May 2025 and is expected to be scaled across provinces.

14.	Notwithstanding challenges, 67 foreign ex-combatants were repatriated by the Mission, in collaboration with the Rwanda Demobilization and Reintegration Commission, from Goma to Rwanda. MONUSCO, in anticipation of increased demand for repatriations in the context of the evolving peace process, also initiated the rehabilitation of its Goma transit camp and the construction of a new transit site in Sake. Following the capture of Goma by AFC/M23 in January 2025, the Mission supplied rations and life support and essential items to national defence and security forces sheltering in its premises.

15.	MONUSCO provided political and technical support for the adoption of a national security sector reform plan and road map and the establishment of a security sector reform monitoring commission. MONUSCO also signed a joint workplan with the National Security Council to provide technical support for the gradual operationalization of the newly established security sector reform monitoring commission and the formulation of a national security policy. Furthermore, the Mission supported the defence and security committee of the National Assembly in their review of the draft of the police programming law (2026–2030) ahead of its adoption.

16.	Strategic communication, including through Radio Okapi, remained central to countering disinformation and hate speech, and to fostering trust in State institutions and peace processes. At the national level, the Mission’s sustained engagement contributed to the resubmission of the draft law against tribalism, racism and xenophobia, which MONUSCO continued to advocate as a vital tool to counter rising intercommunity tensions and political intolerance. These efforts, undertaken in close collaboration with the United Nations country team and international partners, have advanced peace consolidation, promoted inclusive governance and reinforced regional stability amid persistent security, financial and operational challenges.

17.	MONUSCO identified and prevented the recruitment into the national defence and security forces of 103 minors, including 12 girls. This was achieved through the screening of 8,860 candidates, including 69 women. More than 700 children, including more than 200 girls, were voluntarily released by armed groups following advocacy by MONUSCO, United Nations agencies and partners. Training and awareness-raising activities were conducted for 1,627 elements of the national defence and security forces as well as for non-State armed groups.

18.	MONUSCO supported the SADC Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo until its withdrawal in June 2025, providing technical, logistical and human-rights compliance support through high-level meetings, technical sessions and training. The Mission also provided medical support, including evacuation, treatment and surgery, and the repatriation of human remains.

19.	In South Kivu, the Mission, the country team, the Government, civil society and non-governmental organizations (NGOs) jointly coordinated the implementation of the provincial transition road map, aligning resources to meet core transition requirements. The support plan for the transition in South Kivu focused on support for maintaining early warning, child protection and community-based protection mechanisms; strengthening State authority, and the efforts to combat impunity and upholding the rule of law; maintaining human rights monitoring; and supporting the disarmament, demobilization and reintegration programme following the disengagement of MONUSCO.

20.	On the basis of lessons learned, the Mission worked with the Congolese authorities to adapt the disengagement approach for North Kivu and Ituri, shifting away from a province-by-province disengagement to a localized approach, taking into account the protection risks and security conditions on the ground. A joint monitoring mission between the United Nations and government representatives in South Kivu helped to identify progress, challenges and lessons learned six months after the withdrawal of MONUSCO.

21.	The significant deterioration in the security situation in January and February 2025 in North Kivu and South Kivu affected the implementation of the transition plan in South Kivu. To meet evolving requirements and mitigate risks to civilians, the United Nations system reprogrammed transition activities for South Kivu under the support plan; activities focused on support for community-based protection mechanisms, child protection and human rights were implemented by five United Nations agencies, funds and programmes.
C.	Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives for 2026/27

22.	In the 2026/27 period, the strategic priorities of MONUSCO are expected to contribute to (a) the protection of civilians in its areas of deployment; (b) the achievement of the objectives set out in Security Council resolution 2773 (2025); and (c) support for the stabilization and strengthening of State institutions in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. In addition to its continuing support for regional and international peace efforts, under Council resolution 2808 (2025) the Mission was formally authorized to support the implementation of a permanent ceasefire. Additional resources are being sought for this activity.

23.	The Mission’s good offices efforts at the regional, national, provincial and local levels are expected to remain essential for MONUSCO to deliver on its core priorities and address underlying drivers of conflict. Given that the AFC/M23 coalition is expected to continue to consolidate territorial control and entrenching parallel administrative structures in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, the Mission will need to sustain a dual-track engagement and mandate implementation strategy that includes continued support for the protection of civilians, stabilization and the reinforcement of State institutions in government-controlled areas, while supporting local mediation and dialogue initiatives, human rights monitoring and coordination in AFC/M23-controlled zones of North Kivu, which remain exceptionally complex environments.

24.	Peace efforts are expected to be anchored in the Washington Accords for Peace and Prosperity between the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Rwanda, and the Doha Framework for a Comprehensive Peace Agreement between the Government of Democratic Republic of the Congo and AFC/M23, complemented by the African Union-led process, and national as well as local dialogues, even though tensions and breaches of the ceasefire are likely to persist on the ground. As peace processes continue to evolve, MONUSCO will adapt its political strategy to support peace efforts and coordination with relevant United Nations entities, the African Union and other partners. This will be complemented by localized dialogue initiatives; the disarmament, resettlement and repatriation of FDLR combatants; support for the disarmament, demobilization and reintegration of Congolese ex-combatants; support for the national security sector and justice reform agendas; continued monitoring and protection of civilians; and broader stabilization efforts.

25.	At the national level, political tensions between the majority, the opposition and civil society are expected to persist ahead of the 2028 elections. Political space is expected to remain constrained, with judicial proceedings deepening political divisions, notwithstanding cabinet adjustments and dialogue mechanisms put forward by the Government to pursue broader political inclusivity. Regional tensions are also likely to continue to fuel instability. These dynamics may influence national dialogue and electoral preparations, requiring sustained United Nations engagement, including through good offices, technical support and coordination.

26.	It is expected that provincial peace mechanisms in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo will need to continue to be strengthened in order to function effectively. MONUSCO will support the consolidation of structured and tailored peace mechanisms that reinforce and restore State authority with local participation, including women and youth associations, while addressing the needs of diverse communities and minorities. MONUSCO will also continue to provide political and logistical support to African peace processes, following the merger of the Nairobi and Luanda processes, to address the issue of armed groups as part of the effort to advance inclusive peace processes.

27.	In Ituri, MONUSCO will continue to support the Ituri permanent dialogue mechanism, enhancing its monitoring of peace processes, including the Aru I and II dialogues among armed groups and working with national authorities to secure its institutionalization. Local mediation mechanisms will remain essential for direct engagement with armed groups and communities, creating conditions conducive to disarmament, demobilization and reintegration and complementing regional and international processes. The Mission will strengthen coordination with locally led peacebuilding initiatives, invest in national platforms such as the Peacebuilding Community of Practice, and expand partnerships with interfaith actors, women’s groups and youth networks in government-held areas. The MONUSCO force will sustain its engagement through coordinated operational support for FARDC, and through established deconfliction and coordination arrangements with the Ugandan People’s Defence Forces (UPDF), including liaison, information-sharing and preventive measures to reduce the risk of escalation.

28.	While continuing to advocate for the full removal of restrictions on its freedom of movement and operations in areas under AFC/M23 control, the Mission will maintain its engagement with AFC/M23 to preserve its ability to deliver on its mandate to protect civilians and to monitor and report human rights abuses in North Kivu. The Mission will provide technical expertise, logistical support, analysis and security assistance for the implementation of the Washington Accords for Peace and Prosperity, the Doha Framework and African Union-led peace processes, including in support of a permanent ceasefire.

29.	Notwithstanding ongoing peace processes, the overall security environment is expected to remain highly volatile, shaped by the continued presence and fragmentation of armed groups, competing regional interests and the limited reach of State authority. The Mission’s ability to deliver on its mandate will depend on sustained reinforcement of its force and protection of civilian capacities, strengthened coordination with regional security actors and effective integration of political processes as the Mission continues to work towards its end state, which is to reduce the threat posed by armed groups to a level that can be managed by the Congolese authorities.

30.	It is expected that in Ituri, intercommunal violence driven by the Coalition des démocrates congolais, Zaïre, and Convention révolutionnaire populaire will persist, with recurrent attacks undermining stabilization initiatives such as the Aru II dialogue and the disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and stabilization programme. The Allied Democratic Forces (ADF) armed group is expected to remain one of the most serious threats to civilians in Ituri and North Kivu, including by continuing killings, abductions and causing mass displacement. The capacity of national and regional actors to dismantle ADF or substantially reduce its impact during the 2026/27 period will remain limited, reinforcing the need for joint protection interventions between MONUSCO and the national defence and security forces, as well as the sustained mobility of the MONUSCO force for a robust protection of civilians and enhanced situational awareness.

31.	In North Kivu, clashes between AFC/M23, supported by the Rwanda Defence Force, and Wazalendo militias, as well as operations against the FDLR, are likely to generate high civilian casualties and displacement. While the processes under the Washington Accords for Peace and Prosperity and the Doha Framework may offer opportunities for de-escalation and improved humanitarian access, their effectiveness will hinge on consistent implementation and coordinated engagement, which the Mission must plan to support. In Lubero, it is anticipated that Operation Shujaa, a joint operation of UPDF and FARDC, will maintain a scope that extends beyond counter-ADF operations, with coordination between MONUSCO and the UPDF expected to be strengthened gradually over the 2026/27 period.

32.	Against this backdrop, and in the context of continued efforts to prepare the ground for a successful transition from MONUSCO, the Mission will scale up efforts to reinforce the capacity of the Congolese national police and support community-based policing efforts, including through joint patrols, training and support for rapid protection responses, with a specific focus on urban centres such as Beni and Bunia. These efforts are expected to reinforce State authority, strengthen civilian protection – including against sexual and gender-based violence, conflict-related sexual violence and child rights violations – and build community trust through engagement, early warning, strengthened rule of law and efforts to combat impunity.

33.	In areas under AFC/M23 control, unless there is an agreement reached through the process under the Doha Framework, parallel administrations are expected to consolidate territorial presence and governance structures, resulting in continued challenges related to service delivery. In Goma, the Mission’s continued communication with AFC/M23 will be critical to ensure the freedom of movement of MONUSCO, the protection of civilians, notably through human rights monitoring, and wider progress made in regional peace efforts.

34.	The Mission’s approach to transition planning, which will continue to be based on a geographical and thematic prioritization of tasks based on the local dynamics and capacities, will place the Government at the centre and will continue to be dependent on close collaboration between the United Nations and Congolese authorities. Emphasis will also be placed on strengthened collaboration between the Mission and United Nations agencies, funds and programmes, notably through joint programmes.

35.	Protection of civilians will remain at the core of the MONUSCO mandate, implemented through a comprehensive approach combining joint operations with FARDC and the Congolese National Police for physical protection and deterrence, alongside efforts to address the root causes of conflict. The Mission will continue to strengthen the capacities of FARDC, the Congolese National Police and civilian actors, including the Protection Civile, alongside governance and accountability reforms. The Mission will also support the progressive transfer of early warning and response systems and enhance civilian-military cooperation, which will remain central to enhancing local resilience. Unarmed civilian protection initiatives will empower communities to prevent, mitigate and respond to threats through non-violent, locally driven mechanisms.

36.	The MONUSCO force will align resources and concentrate capabilities where the impact on protection of civilians is greatest. Adopting a more dynamic and mobile posture, with fewer static bases, the force will advance “protection through projection”. Close coordination with national authorities, regional partners, United Nations police and civilian components will ensure integrated and agile responses to emerging threats. The Mission will sustain strong advocacy to prevent attacks on civilians and partners in the protection of civilians. These measures will be critical to preserving civic space, reinforcing local resilience, and sustaining national and regional peace efforts in the 2026/27 period.

37.	MONUSCO will contribute to the disarmament, resettlement and repatriation of FDLR combatants as part of the FDLR neutralization plan. The Mission will continue to support the Government in the implementation of the disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and stabilization programme to address armed group threats, with a focus on building the capacity of national, provincial and local authorities in disarming, demobilizing and reintegrating combatants through a community-based approach. Good offices will remain essential to fostering voluntary disarmament; promoting dialogue, mediation and confidence-building; and creating conditions for the return of State authority. Community violence reduction and weapons and ammunition management projects will be used to reduce local-level violence. The Mission will also support reconciliation processes, including transitional justice initiatives. Disarmament, demobilization, repatriation, reintegration and resettlement efforts for foreign combatants will be sustained through awareness-raising, logistical support, and repatriation assistance, including the management of transit sites, in coordination with the Office of the Special Envoy of the Secretary-General for the Great Lakes Region.

38.	Community-based reinsertion and stabilization projects will promote livelihoods and social cohesion in coordination with United Nations agencies and partners. Disarmament, demobilization and reintegration efforts will also remain linked to counter-terrorism objectives to address groups designated as terrorist organizations. Transition planning will also include assessing national disarmament, demobilization and reintegration and stabilization capacities, coordinating United Nations system support and identifying lead entities to support various thematic areas for a gradual transfer of tasks. The Mission will use its convening role to mobilize non-United Nations partners and ensure sustainability, in particular by promoting access to employment, education and social protection as key elements of the successful reintegration of former combatants.

39.	MONUSCO will continue to use its good offices and technical support to foster consensus on governance reforms, prioritizing the security sector. Efforts will focus on consolidating institutional reforms, strengthening State capacities and advancing a nationally owned and people-centred security sector reform process in coordination with the United Nations country team and non-United Nations partners. The inclusion of women, young people and persons with disabilities will remain a cross-cutting priority.

40.	The Mission will enhance synergies between security sector reform efforts, disarmament, demobilization and reintegration processes, and peace processes, including through the vetting of ex-combatants for their possible integration into security institutions and managing related risks, coordinated through the National Monitoring Commission on security sector reform and engagement with FARDC, the Congolese National Police and the National Security Council. The Mission will further engage with the Parliament and civil society to enhance civilian oversight of the security sector and the ongoing reform process.

41.	The Mission will support judicial authorities to combat impunity and uphold the rule of law. Priorities will include building capacity to address serious crimes, expanding digital investigation and case management, reinforcing regional judicial cooperation in coordination with the Office of the Special Envoy for the Great Lakes, and reducing prison overcrowding through mobile courts and inspections. MONUSCO will support the implementation of the Police Programming Act by providing technical expertise and advisory support to the Congolese National Police. Corrections support will consolidate progress under the 2023 prison law, focusing on oversight, infrastructure rehabilitation, the humane management of detainees and the security of high-risk prisoners. Together with the United Nations Joint Human Rights Office in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and the United Nations country team, MONUSCO will sustain joint support through the police and justice reform programmes, with an emphasis on combating impunity, modernizing and professionalizing the police, protecting victims and witnesses, and strengthening accountability as part of the transition.

42.	Human rights violations and abuses are expected to remain significant, in particular in conflict-affected provinces, requiring sustained monitoring, investigations and reporting. Direct and mobile monitoring will be maintained across the Mission’s area of operations, alongside strengthened partnerships with national institutions, NGOs and civil society organizations to reinforce protection capacity.

43.	Conflict-related sexual violence is likely to remain widespread, perpetrated by both State and non-State actors. MONUSCO will continue prevention, monitoring and response efforts in cooperation with specialized NGOs and victims’ associations, ensuring that all support to FARDC and the Congolese National Police remains in strict compliance with the United Nations human rights due diligence policy). Implementation of the FARDC road map for delisting from conflict-related sexual violence parties and the Congolese National Police action plan (2025–2029) will require continued technical support, training, and accountability measures.

44.	Public information campaigns are expected to be scaled up for prevention of human rights violations and awareness-raising, including in the context of the implementation of tasks for the protection of civilians, as well as disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and transitional justice. MONUSCO will strengthen accountability by supporting human rights defenders and NGOs in monitoring commitments with treaty bodies and under the universal periodic review, expanding remote monitoring in AFC/M23-controlled areas and consolidating emergency assistance for victims.

45.	MONUSCO is likely to continue to face a hostile information landscape shaped by scepticism towards international organizations and frustration over persistent insecurity. The Mission will reconfigure Radio Okapi to support its sustainability and legacy, including through strengthened national capacity-building for community journalists, expanded youth and local-language programming, and enhanced digital media capabilities. In parallel, the Mission will continue its initiatives to counter hate speech and promote social cohesion, while supporting national institutions on legislative reforms against tribalism, racism and xenophobia.

46.	MONUSCO will continue to implement the United Nations zero-tolerance policy on sexual exploitation and abuse through a three-pronged approach: (a) prevention through awareness-raising initiatives and regular risk assessments at all mission locations; (b) immediate response to allegations of misconduct; and (c) victim assistance.
Planning assumptions for ceasefire monitoring and verification

47.	Under Security Council resolution 2808 (2025), MONUSCO is authorized to support the implementation of a permanent ceasefire, including through participation in the Ceasefire Oversight and Verification Mechanism and the provision of technical and logistical support to the Expanded Joint Verification Mechanism Plus. Such support will be undertaken within the parameters of the Mission’s existing status-of-forces agreement, and subject to the consent and continued cooperation of the parties to the ceasefire. This includes freedom of movement for MONUSCO personnel in all mandated areas, unhindered access to designated areas and lines agreed by the parties, and the safety and security of United Nations personnel engaged in ceasefire-related tasks. The Mission’s support for ceasefire monitoring and verification will remain closely integrated with broader political engagement, strategic communications and reporting frameworks, strictly within the scope of its ceasefire mandate and without extending to its functions on the protection of civilians or State-authority support in South Kivu. In addition, MONUSCO will strengthen its good offices in supporting and undertaking local dialogues to prevent the escalation of violence and counter hate speech; provide advice and assistance to inclusive peace processes; and provide strategic and technical advice on critical reforms designed to advance peace processes.

48.	The Mission’s engagement in ceasefire monitoring, including in South Kivu, will be phased, with the scale and intensity of activities calibrated to the level of participation agreed by the parties and endorsed by the Security Council. Initial planning therefore reflects active, field-based monitoring within the current authorized troop ceiling, recognizing that any expansion beyond existing capacities will require additional force generation and resource approval.

49.	MONUSCO will, to the extent possible, leverage existing infrastructure, assets and support arrangements in North Kivu and Ituri, while retaining an ability to scale, reconfigure or redeploy to support ceasefire monitoring and verification activities in South Kivu, as explicitly authorized in resolution 2808 (2025). In South Kivu, such activities are assumed to be limited exclusively to ceasefire-related functions and will not entail the protection of civilians. Accordingly, construction and maintenance requirements are envisaged to support the Expanded Joint Verification Mechanism Plus, including for camp infrastructure and enabling services such as power generation, water and sanitation, waste management, and related support, commensurate with ceasefire monitoring needs.

50.	Ceasefire monitoring will be conducted in a challenging and fluid security environment, and monitoring and verification activities will rely on a combination of mobile deployments, liaison engagement, remote monitoring and layered mobility solutions, including air assets, as well as intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance capabilities. Adequate force protection, medical support (including casualty and medical evacuation), secure communications and information management arrangements will be essential enablers. Dedicated unmanned aerial systems will support cost-effective ceasefire monitoring by extending coverage, enabling timely verification and reducing exposure of personnel in high-risk or inaccessible areas. These capabilities will operate in complementarity with existing ground and aerial assets to ensure effective coverage across the full ceasefire monitoring area, in recognition that no single platform can provide comprehensive coverage over such a vast and diverse operational terrain.

51.	The introduction of ceasefire monitoring tasks will generate resource requirements beyond the Mission’s current deployment level, particularly in light of the repatriation of uniformed capabilities carried out in 2025. Consequently, additional requirements to support the ceasefire monitoring and verification mandate will need to be met in full in order to avoid undermining the Mission’s core priorities relating to the protection of civilians and stabilization.

children.text: Mission support initiatives

52.	In anticipation of continued operational challenges, the Mission has realigned its approach, integrating greater flexibility and a responsive posture in order to ensure the continuity of critical functions across geographically dispersed locations during the 2025/26 period. Workforce and resource planning will continue to be driven by the need to sustain essential logistics, service delivery and operational support capacities, particularly in high-risk and priority areas, in a dynamic environment driven by the MONUSCO force’s operations being more agile and mobile, as part of the protection through projection approach.

53.	Mission support will remain focused on delivering agile, efficient and timely services to military, police and substantive components. The Mission has now established its main supply chain and mobility hub in Entebbe, Uganda, to ensure rapid response to emerging needs in a safer environment that is conducive to the welfare and well-being of personnel and the security of assets and inventories. This will also better position the Mission for disengagement at the appropriate time. The Mission will sustain its logistical support for the forward force headquarters in Beni to facilitate joint operations and strengthened coordination with FARDC, while remaining agile and flexible to expand its areas of support to South Kivu, pursuant to Security Council resolution 2808 (2025).

54.	MONUSCO will intensify efforts to enhance cost-effectiveness in a constrained financial environment by tightening controls on inventories and reducing ground and air transport operations and fuel consumption. The ongoing asset optimization programme and strengthened oversight of aviation operations through improved logistics, financial management, vendor performance and flight-hour utilization, will reinforce accountability and ensure efficient, mission-aligned service delivery throughout the 2026/27 period. Personnel welfare will remain a priority, with continued investment in safety, camp management and environmental sustainability. This includes contributions to the United Nations environmental strategy for peace operations for 2023–2030, focusing on renewable energy use and waste reduction. The Mission is contributing to the reduction of its environmental footprint by increasing its mobile operations for enhanced protection through projection and reducing the reliance on permanent and temporary operating bases.

55.	MONUSCO will continue to implement measures to strengthen the safety and security of all personnel through the enhancement of mission facilities and infrastructure. Priority will be given to gender-sensitive accommodation, in particular for uniformed personnel deployed in high-risk areas. Perimeter security will be reinforced through the expansion of closed-circuit television surveillance systems, the installation of physical barriers and regular updates of security risk assessments in line with Department for Safety and Security standards. The Mission will also maintain efforts to rehabilitate key roads and bridges to ensure force mobility and sustain reliable supply chain routes across its areas of operation.

56.	The proposed budget for 2026/27 represents a significant recalibration of posture for MONUSCO which started in 2024/25 and continued in the 2025/26 period. As a result of the M23/AFC territorial expansions in parts of North Kivu, the Mission’s presence in North Kivu was adjusted; the MONUSCO force headquarters were relocated to Beni and operations were concentrated in areas where the Mission’s freedom of movement was not affected. The Mission reorganized its presence around the area of operations to provide agile and responsive support to all emerging requirements, ensuring that all regions were equally and effectively serviced.

57.	Subsequently, the Security Council, in its resolution 2808 (2025), introduced a new mandate priority for MONUSCO to support the implementation of Council resolution 2773 (2025), with a focus on support for a permanent ceasefire. This necessitated a reversal of the budgetary reductions that had been initially considered as well as the reinstatement of mission uniformed capabilities. Given that the Mission’s engagement in support of ceasefire monitoring will be phased (see paras. 47 to 51 above), there may also be a need to further adjust its operations, including mobile deployments, liaison engagements monitoring and verification tasks, as well as for potential deployment to South Kivu in accordance with decisions of the Council and as a result of the Mission’s full participation in the Ceasefire Oversight and Verification Mechanism.

58.	The Mission will undertake a comprehensive package of cost optimization, process optimization and allocative efficiency measures in the 2026/27 period. Key initiatives include: (a) extending the life cycle of heavy maintenance and refurbishment of transport equipment and infrastructure assets; (b) outsourcing of electrical and mechanical maintenance work, thereby reducing a reliance on staff and non-staff resources; (c) investing in hydropower connectivity and generator synchronization to reduce recurrent fuel and maintenance costs; and (d) significantly rationalizing air operations through fleet reductions, where possible, and reduced flight hours. In parallel, the Mission will leverage technology and streamline processes through the optimization and reconfiguration of Internet service provisioning and the consolidation of service contracts. These measures reflect a shift away from high overhead costs, consistent with the principles of the UN80 Initiative on cost discipline.

59.	The proposed reduction in resources has been achieved by analyzing all the existing mission resources in the new political and security context, including the non-permissive environment in the M23-controlled areas, and shifting and reprofiling resources to support the new operational context. Moreover, after the implementation of the contingency plan, another assessment was performed to identify efficiencies and to maximize the opportunities provided by this exercise. In this regard, the Mission has recalibrated its operational posture and continues to do so to prioritize high-risk areas and core mandated tasks, while strengthening internal controls, reconfiguring structures and adapting delivery modalities. The Mission will therefore implement organizational reconfiguration and functional streamlining aimed at reducing costs while preserving critical operational capacity. The redesign of the staffing structure emphasizes flatter reporting lines, the consolidation and streamlining of roles and the merging of functions with complementary responsibilities. These measures include the nationalization of 42 posts and the abolishment of 150 positions and the redeployment and reassignment of staff and functions to other duty stations, notably Entebbe, which is a lower-cost duty station in an environment of enhanced safety and well-being.
D.	Regional mission cooperation

60.	During the 2024/25 period, the Mission supported the Government’s efforts to enhance political and security cooperation in the Great Lakes region by collaborating with the United Nations Regional Office for Central Africa and the Office of the Special Envoy for the Great Lakes. Both Offices shared information to consolidate peace and help to prevent conflict in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo. The Mission continued to support the resident coordinator offices in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda.

61.	The Mission continued to manage the Entebbe Support Base and provide logistical and technical support services and goods to the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe (RSCE) and other tenants. The Mission continued to provide services such as utilities, fuel, aviation, maintenance, medical support and general supplies on a cost-recovery basis, based on the number of staff of each entity present at the base. The Mission also assisted several field missions conducted by regional entities through aviation and aviation safety resources, including refuelling at Entebbe International Airport on a cost-recovery basis.

62.	During the 2026/27 period, MONUSCO, the Office of the Special Envoy for the Great Lakes, the United Nations Regional Office for Central Africa and the United Nations Office to the African Union will provide complementary support for the implementation of the Washington Accords for Peace and Prosperity, the Doha Framework and African Union-led peace initiatives, in line with their mandates and comparative advantages. Their contributions will include good offices, mediation support, technical advice on security arrangements and disarmament, demobilization and reintegration, promotion of regional economic integration, and strengthened coordination with the African Union, the East African Community, the Economic Community of Central African States, SADC and the International Conference on the Great Lakes Region.

63.	It is further assumed that the success of regional peace efforts will depend on coordination across United Nations entities, including on regional threat and conflict analysis, engagement with civil society, including women and youth, and mobilization of international partners to sustain political momentum, resource mobilization and accountability mechanisms throughout the implementation phase of the Washington Accords for Peace and Prosperity.

64.	The Mission will continue to manage the MONUSCO Entebbe Support Base and provide logistical and technical support services to RSCE and other tenants located on the Entebbe Campus. RSCE will remain a key partner in supporting the Mission’s administrative and financial operations, including those related to onboarding and separation processes, benefits and payroll management, vendor payments, entitlements, official travel and claims processing. The Mission will also continue leveraging the Global Procurement Support Section (GPSS) in Entebbe to strengthen regional procurement efficiency through joint acquisition planning and consolidated systems contracts.

F.	Proposed staffing changes

65.	The structure and footprint of MONUSCO have been brought in line with current operational realities to ensure that the Mission’s core mandated priorities are delivered efficiently and that it has greater flexibility and a responsive posture to guarantee the continuity of critical functions across geographically dispersed locations. The Mission’s presence in Goma and Kinshasa has been recalibrated, and its operational capacity in Beni, Bunia and Entebbe has been reinforced to ensure streamlined, efficient and responsive support for existing and new mandate implementation, in particular in high-risk and priority areas.

66.	Efficiency gains will be pursued through the adjustment of the Mission’s footprint, and the optimization of logistics and supply chain systems. The Mission will continue to progressively nationalize functions to strengthen national capacity-building and enhance cost effectiveness.
Abolishment

67.	It is proposed that 150 posts and positions (1 D-1, 1 P-5, 1 P-4, 2 P-3, 9 Field Service, 6 National Professional Officer, 105 national General Service, 7 United Nations Volunteer and 18 general temporary assistance national General Service) be abolished. The abolishments were identified as efficiencies or pertaining to residual capacity to be phased out in line with the Mission’s exit from South Kivu and the closure of the Goma airport. The list of posts and positions proposed to be abolished are presented in table 2.
Conversions

68.	It is proposed that 42 international posts (2 P-4, 7 P-3, 6 P-2, 10 Field Service and 17 international United Nations Volunteer) be nationalized to 15 National Professional Officer, 10 national General Service and 17 national United Nations Volunteer, in line with the MONUSCO transition plan. A core component of the Mission transition strategy is the progressive nationalization of posts to strengthen national capacities and ensure sustainability beyond the Mission’s presence. The posts proposed to be nationalized include functions in the areas of administration, finance, geospatial information management, human rights, information management, property management, political affairs, procurement, public information, security and transport. The list of posts proposed to be converted are presented in table 2.
Reclassifications
69.	It is proposed that five positions be reclassified downward as follows:
(a)	One Chief of Aviation Safety (P-5) in Goma, to Aviation Safety Officer (P-4) in Kinshasa, in line with the reduced scope of aviation operations in the Mission;
(b)	One Civil Affairs Officer (P-4), to Civil Affairs Officer (P-3) in Goma, in line with the drawdown of activities and reduced field presence in the Goma area of responsibility;
(c)	One HIV/AIDS Officer (P-4) in Goma, to HIV/AIDS Officer (P-3) in Entebbe;
(d)	One Administrative Officer (P-4) in Goma, to Administrative Officer (P-3) in Entebbe;
(e)	One Fuel Officer (P-4), to Logistics Officer (P-3) in Goma, in line with the drawdown of activities and reduced field presence in the Goma area of responsibility.
70.	The list of posts proposed to be reclassified are presented in table 2.
Reassignments
71.	It is proposed that four positions be reassigned as follows:

(a)	One Senior Political Affairs Officer in Goma (P-5) as Senior Human Rights Officer in Beni, to lead, sustain and strengthen the Mission’s prevention and response to conflict-related sexual violence. This role will ensure continued leadership and technical oversight of interventions relating to conflict-related sexual violence, reinforce coordination mechanisms under the monitoring, analysis and reporting arrangements, and maintain the momentum of the Government’s renewed commitments under the road maps on conflict-related sexual violence adopted by the Ministry of Defence and the Ministry of Interior. The position is therefore critical to ensuring the Mission’s sustained engagement in supporting national accountability and prevention frameworks in line with the 2013 joint communiqué signed between the United Nations and the Democratic Republic of the Congo and its addendum, in particular in view of the escalating violence and increasing incidents of conflict-related sexual violence in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo;
(b)	One Procurement Officer (P-3) in Goma as Staff Counsellor in Entebbe, to promote the physical, mental and emotional well-being of MONUSCO staff members who moved to Entebbe;

(c)	One Administrative Assistant (Field Service) in Kinshasa as Training Assistant, to support the Integrated Mission Training Centre’s ability to deliver expanded training opportunities and ensure the preparedness of personnel for future career phases, both within and beyond the mission life cycle;

(d)	One Air Operations Assistant (Field Service) in Goma, as Training Assistant, to support the Integrated Mission Training Centre’s ability to deliver expanded training opportunities and ensure preparedness of personnel for future career phases, both within and beyond the mission life cycle.
72.	The list of posts proposed to be reassigned are presented in table 2.
Redeployments
73.	It is proposed that 132 positions be redeployed as follows:

(a)	One Head of Political Affairs (D-1) from the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator) to the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General to support mission-wide coordination, which links mandate implementation across all mission components to advance transition and strengthen its impact, effectiveness and accountability. The post proposed to be redeployed is presented in table 2;

(b)	A total of 119 posts from the Mission’s duty stations in the Democratic Republic of the Congo to Entebbe, to improve responsiveness to emerging needs through the consolidation of support functions in Entebbe, mitigate operational risks and further strengthen the expanded role of MONUSCO as a strategic logistics and support hub, including enhanced readiness for disengagement. Since its inception, the Entebbe Support Base has served as the Mission’s main logistics hub, supporting the transit of troops, police units and goods into the eastern part of the Democratic Republic of the Congo. The list of posts proposed for redeployment to Entebbe are presented in table 3;

(c)	A total of 12 posts from Entebbe to duty stations in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, to recalibrate the Mission’s presence across key duty stations. The list of posts proposed for redeployment from Entebbe are presented in table 4.
Continuation of general temporary assistance positions
74.	The continuation of 21 general temporary assistance positions is proposed, as follows:

(a)	One Principal Coordination Officer (D-1), in the Stabilization Support Unit, to serve as the Mission’s main interlocutor for the national strategy for the implementation of the disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and stabilization programme for ex-combatants. The Principal Coordination Officer will coordinate support for the national programme among partners of the United Nations system, national authorities and international partners, including Member States; undertake high-level political engagements, with frequent interactions with the President’s Office, the Prime Minister’s Office and ambassadors; provide strategic advice to the Government; and manage the Stabilization Coherence Fund;

(b)	One Senior Judicial Affairs Officer (P-5), to ensure the sustainability of the achievements of the justice sector under the Joint Justice Reform Support Programme, enabling the sustained strategic engagement of the Mission with the Programme and undertaking planning and programmatic work, both for the current programme and for the development of a successor programme to be anchored in the United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Framework for the period 2025–2029;

(c)	One Programme Management Officer (P-4), in the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Protection and Operations), to direct the implementation of the MONUSCO mine action programme;

(d)	One Legal Officer (P-3), to assist in the disciplinary processes and financial recovery relating to suspected medical insurance fraud cases and to defend the Organization before the United Nations Dispute Tribunal with respect to the imposition of disciplinary and financial recovery measures;

(e)	Five positions of Disarmament, Demobilization and Reintegration Officer (National Professional Officer) and 12 positions of Disarmament, Demobilization and Reintegration Assistant (national General Service) to support disarmament, demobilization, repatriation, reintegration and resettlement processes to return foreign combatants in the context of the broader peace process, and to support the Government in the implementation of the disarmament, demobilization, community recovery and stabilization programme. The positions will support strategic liaison, awareness-raising and extraction functions, weapons and ammunition management, screening and documentation, management of transit camps, medical screening, repatriation coordination, and the capacity building of national, provincial and local authorities in the disarmament, demobilization and reintegration of Congolese combatants, including the management of community violence reduction interventions and community reinsertion projects.

children.text: Evaluation activities

75.	The following evaluations completed by the Office of Internal Oversight Services (OIOS) during the 2024/25 period have guided MONUSCO planning for the 2026/27 period: (a) Evaluation of the contribution of strategic communications to fostering public trust in peacekeeping operations (9 August 2024); (b) Triennial review of the implementation of recommendations in the OIOS evaluation report on the effectiveness of human rights monitoring, reporting and follow-up in the United Nations multi-dimensional peacekeeping operations (1 October 2024); (c) Strengthening the role of evaluation and the application of evaluation findings on programme design, delivery and policy directives for 2022–2023 (18 February 2025); (d) Evaluation of Children and Armed Conflict in Peacekeeping Operations (27 June 2025); (e) Triennial Review Report on the Implementation of OIOS Recommendations in the Report on the Evaluation of Political Affairs in Peacekeeping Missions (30 June 2025); and (f) Evaluation of Regional and Multilateral Partnerships for Peacekeeping (24 September 2025).

76.	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, MONUSCO has integrated key findings, lessons learned and recommendations into the planning for the 2026/27 budget period. The evaluations guided adjustments aimed at enhancing mandate delivery, strategic coherence and accountability. Specifically, MONUSCO has strengthened strategic communications to foster public trust, counter misinformation and improve the transparency of peacekeeping operations; enhanced coordination and effectiveness of human rights monitoring, reporting and follow-up through closer collaboration with the country office of the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights and national institutions; mainstreamed evaluation findings into programme design and delivery, ensuring evidence-based planning and stronger linkages between outcomes, outputs and performance indicators; improved the integration of political analysis, human rights and protection strategies across Mission components, in line with recommendations from the triennial OIOS reviews; deepened partnerships with regional and multilateral actors to promote coherence and burden-sharing in peacekeeping and political engagement; and reinforced child protection mechanisms and monitoring capacities within Mission operations to strengthen prevention, reporting and response efforts relating to grave violations against children.

77.	Subject to the availability of resources, the Mission will conduct a self-evaluation on at least one of the following areas: risk management and organizational resilience framework, inter-pillar integration and joint planning and programme management, and the Mission’s environmental footprint and greening strategy.

82.	Credits comprising the unencumbered balances, other revenue and the cancellation of prior-period obligations reduce the assessed contributions owed by Member States in the following financial year. The credit for the 2024/25 period for overall peacekeeping operations amounts to $335.5 million, including the support entities and the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali (MINUSMA). In the current financial situation, offsetting this large amount of credit against a future assessment for the 2026/27 period will reduce the collection and undermine the ability of peacekeeping operations to fully implement their mandates. Accordingly, the Secretary-General proposes that the General Assembly approve that the credits in respect of the financial period 2024/25 be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the implementation of those mandates; and allow Member States to apply their share towards current or future dues once the Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has sufficiently improved. This proposal is explained in more detail in the peacekeeping overview report.

85.	The vacancy factors take into account the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27. Accordingly, the proposed vacancy rates for civilian personnel categories with abolishments were estimated using the total number of vacant posts and positions as at 31 December 2025, net of the number of posts and positions proposed for abolishment. For categories without proposed abolishments, the standard policy guidance was applied, using either the 12-month average vacancy rate from January to December 2025 or the actual vacancy rate as at 31 December 2025. For military and police personnel, the vacancy factors reflect their planned deployment during the period. This approach ensures that vacancy rates are based on, to the extent possible, the actual data while maintaining consistency across the proposed budgets. A vacancy rate of 50 per cent has been applied in the calculation of costs for the proposed conversions.

86.	The flight hour utilization rates for the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25, the approved and proposed number of flight hours for the 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods, respectively, and the number of flight hours calculated on the basis of the new policy guidance are as follows:

87.	The Mission will deploy 22 aircraft in various locations in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo where the Mission has field offices, Kinshasa and Entebbe, to carry passengers and cargo; for patrols, observation and protection; for troop rotations; for medical and casualty evacuations; and for search and rescue. The proposed number of flight hours takes into account the Mission’s pattern of flight utilization rates. In general, the proposed number of flight hours takes into account the fact that assets deployed in the 2024/25 period could not be utilized in full owing to the conflict in Goma and the subsequent closure of Goma airport. This, together with the hostile air threat over North Kivu and the southern parts of Ituri, prevented the Mission from employing the air fleet in full to meet its operational air support requirements. This affected the helicopter fleet more than the fixed-wing fleet, making it impossible to apply the policy on budgeting for flight hour. The Mission’s air operations have shifted from helicopter-centric to fixed-wing-centric, owing mostly to air threats over North Kivu. This, together with the non-availability of the Goma airport, resulted in the Mission reconfiguring its air fleet and adjusting its flight hour budget to meet the Mission’s critical air support and military air operations requirements.

91.	The proposed resource requirements for the 2026/27 period will amount to $1.0 million. MONUSCO will continue to implement quick-impact projects as a key confidence-building mechanism between the Mission and the Congolese population, in particular in areas where the Mission retains an operational presence. These projects will be jointly designed and executed with local authorities and communities to address urgent needs and support local resilience. Quick-impact projects will prioritize community-driven interventions that respond to urgent local needs and contribute to improved access to basic services, safety and social cohesion. Activities may include the rehabilitation of essential infrastructure, enhancements to public safety measures, and initiatives that strengthen local resilience and trust between communities and the Mission. Additional initiatives will include support for vocational training centres, the rehabilitation of water points and psychosocial assistance for survivors of sexual and gender-based violence. The Mission will also conduct community awareness-raising and outreach activities to strengthen public understanding and trust in the MONUSCO mandate and the ongoing transition process.

table_content.text: Requests the Secretary-General to improve comprehensive oversight of the activities of peacekeeping missions and implement the recommendations of relevant oversight bodies in this regard to avoid deficiencies in management and related economic losses, with the aim of ensuring full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations (para. 17).

As at 30 September 2025, the Mission had implemented 10 of 17 recommendations issued by the Board of Auditors and 8 of 20 recommendations of OIOS targeted for implementation by the end of 2025. The remaining recommendations are at an advanced stage of implementation, with regular follow-up and monitoring coordinated by the Mission’s compliance and oversight focal points to ensure timely closure. The Mission continues to strengthen its internal control environment through enhanced oversight of administrative, financial and programmatic activities, ensuring full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations. Corrective measures and lessons learned from oversight reviews are systematically integrated into management practices to prevent deficiencies, reduce operational risks and minimize potential economic losses.

Requests that future budget proposals demonstrate the scalability of mission support components, including their staffing and operational costs, be proportionate in relation to the changing level of other mission components and include standard indicators (para. 27).

The mission support ratio (total number of mission support and security personnel per 1,000 total mission personnel) in the 2025/26 period is 95.7, which is lower than the ratio of 103.9 reported for the 2020/21 period.

Requests the Secretary-General to consider options for greater nationalization of functions when formulating budget submissions, commensurate with mission mandates and requirements where applicable (para. 34).

The Mission will continue to review its staffing requirements in line with its priorities and endeavour to nationalize posts whenever feasible. It is proposed that 42 posts and positions (25 international and 17 United Nations Volunteers) be nationalized in the 2026/27 period.

Recalls the provisions of section XVIII of its resolution 61/276, recognizes the important role played by quick-impact projects in establishing and building confidence in missions, further recognizes the importance of conducting assessments of the quick-impact projects regularly, on their needs and impact as required in resolution 61/276, requests the Secretary-General to include the conducted assessments in his next reports, and also requests the Secretary-General to enhance their impact (para. 82).

The Mission continues to conduct regular internal assessments of quick-impact projects through the systematic review of project proposals, progress reports and closure reports during key implementation milestones. These reviews are complemented by field verification missions undertaken prior to project closure to collect stakeholder feedback and directly assess the relevance, effectiveness and sustainability of quick-impact projects on the ground. The most recent assessment, covering such projects implemented during the 2024/25 period, confirmed that the projects continued to play a critical confidence-building role between the Mission, local authorities and communities. Quick-impact projects have contributed to strengthening State authority, improving access to basic services and enhancing community safety in areas where the Mission remains operational. They have also supported social cohesion and reconciliation efforts, in particular through infrastructure improvements, community engagement initiatives and targeted activities that promote the participation and empowerment of women and young people. In line with General Assembly resolution 61/276, the Mission will continue to enhance the impact and sustainability of quick-impact projects by improving performance monitoring, integrating lessons learned into project design, and prioritizing projects that foster local ownership and resilience in the context of the Mission’s ongoing transition.

Financing of the United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo
(A/79/724/Add.9 and General Assembly resolution 79/304)

table_content.text: The Committee also expresses concern regarding the damages to MONUSCO installations and assets and trusts that the results of the ongoing assessment of the damages and detailed information on the full cost of all damage experienced by the Mission will be provided to the General Assembly, in the context of the next proposed budget report (para. 13).

The overall material loss attributable to the AFC/M23 incursion in Goma in January 2025 is assessed at $4.17 million, of which $1.6 million (or 39 per cent), is the subject of an insurance claim that is still under negotiation.
